Nikonenko *, N.O. Chubyr, M.Kh. Urtenov Kuban State University, Membrane Institute, Stavropolskaya Str. 149, 350040 Krasnodar, Russian Federation H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T Limitations of water deionization by electrodialysis (ED) are studied theoretically. 1D and 2D models allowing for water dissociation-recombination are developed. During ED of dilute solutions, space charge arises in the bulk of desalination chamber. This effect is due to recombination of H+ and OH-ions. Water splitting makes impossible total deionization of feed solution. A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Ion-exchange membranes Electrodialysis Concentration polarization Overlimiting current Electroconvection Water dissociation-recombination Water splitting Space charge

citation.title: Mathematical modeling of electrodialysis of a dilute solution with accounting for water dissociation-recombination reactions

content: A B S T R A C T The paper explores the limitations of electrodialysis (ED) in the range of very dilute feed solutions. The results of first principles modeling of this process are presented, taking into account water dissociation-recombination reactions (without catalytic effects). A 1D and a 2D model are formulated and applied. Both models use the equation for the rate of chemical reactions, the Nernst-Planck and Poisson equations; additionally, the NavierStokes equation is used in the 2D model. We show that the dissociation-recombination reactions can cause the appearance of a space charge region (SCR) in the central part of the desalination chamber (DC). In electro­chemistry and colloidal science, the formation of a SCR is associated with the formation of an electrical double layer (EDL) at a charged solid surface. Our modeling predicts that in conditions where the concentrations of H+/ OH-ions and salt ions in solution are comparable, a SCR can appear in the bulk solution due to the recombi­nation of H+ and OH-ions. The latter are generated at the membrane forming the DC (water splitting). Water splitting makes impossible total desalting of aqueous solutions by conventional ED. An inversion of the salt concentration profiles is observed: the salt ions are accumulated near the membranes; a depletion region occurs in the center of the DC. Interplay between the chemical reactions and space charge is discussed.

citation.title: Mathematical modeling of electrodialysis of a dilute solution with accounting for water dissociation-recombination reactions

content: 1. Introduction Electrodialysis (ED) is a widely used membrane process for desali­nation of solutions, concentration and separation of ions [1–8]. The main advantage of ED is that costs decrease approximately in proportion to the decrease in the ion concentration of the feed solution, while in reverse osmosis (RO) and nanofiltration (NF) these costs only slightly depend on the solution concentration [1,8–10]. ED is a less energyconsuming process than RO and NF when the feed solution concentra­tion is sufficiently low: brackish waters and more dilute solutions [11]. However, there are serious limitations of ED related to concentration polarization [11–18]. A sharp decrease in the ion concentration near the membrane surface causes a significant increase in the resistance of the membrane system, which leads to an increase in energy consumption. Electroconvection is considered one of the ways to overcome this limi­tation [9,19–22]. Electroconvection generally occurs as current-induced volume transfer under the action of an electric field applied to a charged solution [23]. During electrodialysis, extended space charge regions (SCR) emerge at the cation-exchange membrane (CEM) and anionexchange membrane (AEM), which form desalination and concentra­tion compartments [24–26]. The thickness of these regions can reach a few μm [17]. An extended SCR can be considered as a nonequilibrium extension of an electrical double layer (EDL) in a near-membrane solu­tion [27].

citation.title: Mathematical modeling of electrodialysis of a dilute solution with accounting for water dissociation-recombination reactions

content: a few μm [17]. An extended SCR can be considered as a nonequilibrium extension of an electrical double layer (EDL) in a near-membrane solu­tion [27]. This (equilibrium) EDL exists even when no current flows through the membrane. Its thickness increases with increasing current density. When the current density is greater than the limiting current density, ilim, a nonequilibrium and unstable addition arises. The action of an external field on the extended SCR causes a microvortex motion of the solution, which facilitates the delivery of fresh solution from the bulk to the membrane surface. As well, the microvortices evacuate the depleted near-membrane solution into the bulk. Electroconvection allows significantly (in several times compared to ilim [28–37]) increasing the mass transfer rate during electrodialysis in the overlimiting current range. In overlimiting current regimes, dissociation of water with the pro­duction of H+ and OH-ions occurs at the depleted solution/membrane interface (the effect is known as water splitting [38]). The cause is the deviation from equilibrium in the reaction H2O ⇄ kd kr H+ + OH-(1) where kd and kr are the rate constants of water dissociation and recombination, respectively. H+ and OH-ions are removed from the interface by the external electric field, which makes the concentration product of these ions lower than the water dissociation (self-ionization) constant, Kw: CHCOH < Kw.

content: rnal electric field, which makes the concentration product of these ions lower than the water dissociation (self-ionization) constant, Kw: CHCOH < Kw. Water splitting has a great influence on the transport processes in membrane systems [16,39–42]. At present, two types of water dissoci­ation are distinguished: (i) non-catalytic reaction of dissociation of water molecules, which occurs in one stage; (ii) catalytic water splitting [38,43–46], which proceeds in the surface layer of ion-exchange mem­branes in several stages with the participation of proton transfer re­actions between charged groups in the membrane and water molecules. In both cases, especially when water dissociation occurs as a noncatalytic reaction, the acceleration of the reaction by a high-strength electric field (the second Wien effect [47–49]) is of great importance [35,38,46,50–56]. The appearance of new charge carriers H+ and OH- ions as a result of water dissociation not only reduces the current effi­ciency during desalination of solutions [57], but also leads to a decrease in space charge in the extended SCR. The latter causes a decrease in electroconvection [58,59]. The effect of water dissociation on ED desalination was considered theoretically, on the basis of the 1D stationary Nernst-Planck-Poisson equation, by Urtenov et al. [60]. A closed no-flow ED desalination compartment limited by a cation-exchange and an anion-exchange membrane initially contained a stagnant NaCl solution was studied.

citation.title: Mathematical modeling of electrodialysis of a dilute solution with accounting for water dissociation-recombination reactions

content: flow ED desalination compartment limited by a cation-exchange and an anion-exchange membrane initially contained a stagnant NaCl solution was studied. It was shown that in a stationary case under a voltage applied to the compartment, the system is in a state when fluxes of salt ions through the membranes are zero. The electric current through the compartment is due to the water dissociation. In this reaction, new charge carriers, H+ and OH-ions are produced, which immediately leave the desalination compartment. The electric current efficiency in such a desalination system is zero. In the present paper, we theoretically predict a new effect, which is due to the coupling between ion electrodiffusion and chemical reactions of water dissociation/recombination during electrodialysis. We show that under deep demineralization conditions, when the concentration of salt ions becomes comparable to the concentration of H+ and OH-ions, rapid recombination reaction can provoke formation of a space charge in the center of the desalination compartment. This space charge gives rise to electroconvection, which interacts with electroconvection initiated by the extended SCR localized near CEM and AEM. The above effects are simulated using a 1D and a 2D nonstationary models. The 1D model, which is perhaps the simplest model that captures the effect of space charge formation in bulk solution, serves to understand the physicochemistry behind this effect.

content: the simplest model that captures the effect of space charge formation in bulk solution, serves to understand the physicochemistry behind this effect. The 2D model illustrates how the space charge in the bulk affects electroconvection in the ED desalination channel. Compared to the previous paper [60], we now account for temporal changes in concentration and electric fields and the evolution of an ED desalination cell from an initial state with a uniform distribu­tion of electrolyte concentration to the stationary state. These changes are especially important in the actual 2D version, which describes spatio-temporal ion concentration and fluid flow patterns in the desa­lination compartment of an electrodialysis cell. 2. Results and discussion 2.1. 1D model 2.1.1. Model formulation A stagnant desalination channel formed by an AEM and a CEM is considered (Fig. 1). At the initial moment of time (t = 0), in the space between the membranes there is an aqueous solution of a binary salt with a concentration of C0. As an example, a solution of potassium chloride is considered; the closeness of the diffusion coefficients of the cation and anion leads to an almost symmetric distribution of concen­trations in the channel. The electrical mode is set by the potential dif­ference Δφ inside the channel between the points x = 0 (boundary with an AEM) and x = H (boundary with a CEM). The value of Δφ increases

content: Desalination 550 (2023) 116398 linearly in time starting from 0 at t = 0. This mode (or linear increase in current) is generally applied when measuring current-voltage charac­teristics. In addition, when modeling in a large range of voltages, the state of the system found at a given t is considered as an initial state when computing its state at t = Δt. As time and Δφ increase, the solution becomes more and more desalted, and the concentrations of K+ and Cl- ions decrease. Along with this, the generation of H+ and OH-ions occurs as a result of the dissociation of water in a depleted solution near the surface of the CEM and AEM (Fig. 1). When a water molecule dissociates near the AEM surface, the released H+ ions are directed into the desa­lination chamber, while the OH-ions leave the reaction zone through the AEM. At the CEM surface, OH-ions, which have arisen during water dissociation, enter the solution in the desalination chamber, and the H+ ions migrate through the CEM. In order to simplify the model and exclude the use of empirical parameters, only the non-catalytic disso­ciation of water in solution is considered in this paper; catalytic re­actions in membranes are not taken into account. For the same reasons, we do not take into account the dependence of the water dissociation rate on the electric field (the second Wien effect).

citation.title: Mathematical modeling of electrodialysis of a dilute solution with accounting for water dissociation-recombination reactions

content: count. For the same reasons, we do not take into account the dependence of the water dissociation rate on the electric field (the second Wien effect). Both the second Wien effect and the catalytic participation of functional groups increase the rate of water splitting [41–46], [61] hence, strengthen the coupling between ion transport and water dissociation/recombination reactions. By neglecting these effects, we simplify the model and eliminate the influence of empirical assumptions. The present model will make it possible to evaluate the effects of water dissociation-recombination re­actions proceeding in ED chambers at the lowest possible rate. It is generally believed that water splitting occurring without catalytic participation of membrane fixed functional groups is negligible [38,46]. However, we will see that even water splitting occurring at the lowest possible rate dramatically affects ED desalination, if the feed concen­tration becomes low enough. In the system under consideration, only the changes in ion concen­trations and other quantities along the transverse coordinate x are important. This allows us to formulate a one-dimensional mathematical problem. Note that the process of solution demineralization in 1D geometry, which occurs in a given volume of solution in time, is similar to what happens in a 2D channel, when we analyze how the situation in the channel cross section changes as a portion of solution moves along the channel.

Although it is impossible to simulate electroconvection (EC) in 1D geometry, it is possible to simulate the formation of the SCR, which is the cause of the EC, as well as the generation and recombination of H+ and OH-ions, which affect the SCRs. At great voltage, it can be of the order of 10-3 C0, as the calculations show (see the next section). We take the values of C2a and C1c equal to C0 to be consistent with the initial conditions (17). It is also important that the values of C2a and C1c have little effect on the distribution of concentrations even at a small distance from the membrane [64]. In fact, the x = 0 and x = H points are localized within the equilibrium part of the EDL; the con­centration distribution there is governed by the Poisson-Boltzmann equation [64]. If we take a higher value for C2a and C1c than C0, the counterion concentration will be equal to C0 at a very small distance from x = 0 (or x = H), which is identified as the membrane surface. As mentioned above, potentiodynamic regime is considered, when the potential difference increases with a certain sweep rate d: φ(t,0) = d ⋅ t. The effect of the sweep rate on the current-voltage curve (CVC) was studied by Moya and Sistat [65]. The potential growth rate should be slow enough to provide a quasi-stationary state at any time. The value of d in our work is chosen as large as possible (to reduce the computation time), but such that, as this value decreases, the concentration profiles and current density at a given potential difference do not depend on d. The mathematical model above [expressed by Eqs. (2)–(19)] is a boundary-value problem for 1D non-stationary system of partial differ­ential equations. The corresponding model belongs to first-principles models, since it is based on the fundamental equations of physics and chemistry and does not involve any empirical relationships and parameters. 2.1.2. Numerical solution The boundary value problem was solved by the finite element method in combination with the parameter continuation method [66]. A non-uniform adaptive grid (containing 2.5 million elements, the length of one element is between 0.2 nm and 0.6 nm) was applied. The grid thickened near the membrane surface and in the desalination compartment bulk in the regions of rapid change of functions.

The idea of the parameter continuation method is in the use of an initial approximation, in which instead of the small (large) values of some input parameters, their values are set to several orders of magnitude larger (less) [66]. The 1D model was implemented using our original software in the Comsol Multiphysics 5.5 environment. It is necessary about 2 h for the computations with one set of input parameters, when the potential drop varies from 0 to 1 V. The used equipment is detailed in the Supplementary materials.

content: 2.2. Analysis of the 1D model 2.2.1. Small degree of desalination Below are the results, unless otherwise stated, for the following pa­rameters: C1c = C2a = 1 mol m-3, d = 0.005 V/s, C0 = 1 mol m-3, C3(0, x) = C3(0,x) = 10-4 mol m-3. In the analysis below, we use two additional functions: 1) ρ = F(C1 -C2 + C3 -C4) is the space charge density. If this function is positive, the total concentration of cations is greater than that of anions, and vice versa. 2) Pw = C3C4/Kw is the reduced ion-product of water. In the region where Pw < 1, dissociation prevails over recombination, and vice versa, recombination dominates when Pw > 1; Pw = 1 signifies that the rates of dissociation and recombination processes are the same, H+ and OH-ions are in equilibrium with water molecules. Numerical analysis of problem (1)–(17) shows that with an increase in the potential difference and simultaneous desalting of the solution, several qualitatively different periods are distinguished. The first period in the range of low Δφ corresponds to the conventional ED regime (described among others by Probstein [23]): the desalination process occurs under conditions when the concentration of salt ions significantly exceeds the concentration of H+ and OH-ions. However, limiting cur­rent density is reached in this regime when Δφ becomes greater than approximately 0.5 V. Typical concentration profiles of salt ions are shown in Fig. 2a.

content: density is reached in this regime when Δφ becomes greater than approximately 0.5 V. Typical concentration profiles of salt ions are shown in Fig. 2a. The salt concentration in the central part of the desalination compartment nearly does not change, while near the membranes the concentration is not far from zero. The salt counterion concentration (that of K+ in Fig. 2b) passes through a minimum where its concentration is about 10-3 C0. A space charge region (SCR), where the concentration of K+ is close to 10-3 mol m-3 while the concentra­tions of Cl-OH-ions is nearly zero, occurs approximately between x =0.98H and x = H. The thickness of this SCR (about 20 μm) is much larger than the thickness of equilibrium EDL (about 10 nm at bulk concen­tration 1 mol m-3). It can be shown that greater part of this SCR is nonequilibrium (the part where the K+ ion concentration decreases with increasing x). This part of the SCR is called extended space charge re­gion; it emerges at overlimiting currents and plays an important role in initiating electroconvection [24,58]. The concentration of H+ and OH-ions is relatively low; the appearance of these ions is due to (non-catalytic) dissociation of water molecules. Under the action of an external electric field, salt ions, as well as H+ and OH-ions, are removed from the near-membrane solution: counterions are transferred through the membrane, co-ions are removed from the near-membrane solution into the bulk solution.

content: near-membrane solution: counterions are transferred through the membrane, co-ions are removed from the near-membrane solution into the bulk solution. A simultaneous decrease in the concentration of both H+ and OH-ions in the nearmembrane solution leads to a decrease in the function Pw, which be­comes <1 in this region, and reaction (1) deviates from equilibrium: dissociation dominates over recombination. At a low degree of desalt­ing, salt ions dominate in all areas of the solution (Fig. 2a and b), the space charge is concentrated near ion-exchange membranes, and the Pw function is less than unity only in thin reaction zones near the AEM and CEM surfaces (Fig. 2c and d). The space charge density is comparatively high, the local maximum in the extended SCR near the CEM reaches about 200 С m-3 (in units of concentration, the value of ρ/F is close to 2 * 10-3 mol m-3) (Fig. 2d). The latter is close to the salt cation con­centration in this region (Fig. 2b). As can be seen from Fig. 2d, the re­action zone (where Pw < 1) is in the same region as the SCR and where there are high field strengths. A similar situation occurs in the bipolar interface layer in a bipolar membrane [61]: a strong electric field at a permselective interface is needed to draw ions H+ and OH-out of the reaction zone and thus shift the dissociation-recombination equilibrium [67]. Coupling between space charge formation and chemical reactions From a mathematical point of view, the appearance of a sharp surge in the function Pw = C3C4/Kw in the middle of the desalination chamber is associated with the presence of a small dimensionless parameter at derivatives ∂Ci/∂t and ∂ji/∂t in Eq. (2) after substituting Eq. (5) into it and dividing the result by the parameter kr, which has a large value. Although such a feature in the behavior of the concentration profiles of H+ and OH-ions in membrane systems is mathematically predictable, it has not been examined in the literature before. With regard to a sharp change in concentrations, space charge density and electric potential at the ion-selective surface of an electrode or membrane and the rela­tionship of this change with a small parameter in the Poisson equation, this feature is well known [63,64,69–71]. The reason for this feature is a sharp change in the concentration of potential-determining ions (the ions, which dominate in the right-hand side of the Poisson equation [Eq. (4)] and, therefore, determine the potential distribution). This change is

content: Desalination 550 (2023) 116398 due to the presence of a fixed charge on the surface and/or in the bulk of one of the phases. The small parameter in the Poisson equation causes a rapid change not only in concentrations, but also in the potential and field strength in a very thin boundary layer: the smaller this parameter, the thinner the boundary EDL. A rapid change in the concentration of potential-determining ions causes the appearance of a strong electric field at the surface of a membrane or electrode. The action of this field on the H+ and OH-ions causes a decrease in the concentration of both of these ions near the interface and causes the fact that function Pw < 1 in the same boundary region where there is a rapid change in φ and ρ. This behavior of electrode and membrane systems, namely a sharp change in the function Pw and ρ near the ion-selective boundary, is known [61,72] and is not surprising. However, the appearance of such a feature in the bulk solution in the volume of the solution is quite unexpected. The reason for the appearance of the SCR at the center of the channel is not the presence of an ion-selective boundary, but the occurrence of a chemical reaction. As a result of this reaction (recombination of the H+ and OH-ions), there is a sharp decrease in the concentration of potential-determining H+ and OH-ions. Since, as noted above, the concentration of salt ions in this reaction zone is comparable to the concentration of H+ and OH-ions (Fig.

Thus, when there is a space charge at an ion-selective interface, the flow of electric current can cause a chemical reaction (in our case, water dissociation). Conversely, we show that when an intensive chemical reaction (recombination of H+ and OH-ions) occurs, it can cause for­mation of an electric space charge in the bulk solution. In both cases, the space charge is due to the occurrence of large concentration gradients. The latter follows from the Nernst-Planck and Poisson equations. The con­centration of salt ions in the central desalted region is about 10-5 mol m-3 (3 * 10-8 mol L-1), that is 10 times lower than the H+ and OH ions' concentration. The desire to further reduce the salt concentration is constrained by a very low current efficiency: the current is mainly used for the formation of H+ and OH-ions in the opposite sides of the desalination compart­ment. These ions move towards each other and recombine in the center of the compartment, which leads to a waste of energy. Moreover, the modeling shows that water splitting makes impossible total desalting of aqueous solutions when using conventional ED. Indeed, H+ ions emitted into the DC accumulate near the anion-exchange membrane in the left part of the chamber. These ions retain salt anions (Cl-ions) (Fig. 5a) in virtue to the requirement of electrical neutrality. Similarly, OH-ions emitted into the DC and accumulated near the cation-exchange mem­brane on the right side of the chamber retain salt cations (K+ ions). The concentration of Cl-and K+ ions captured by the H+ and OH-ions depends on the balance of water dissociation and recombination rates; the higher the current density, the greater the concentrations of H+ and OH-ions, and, accordingly, those of K+ and Cl-ions in the DC.

As time/voltage increases, the space charges at the edges of the central desalted region move away from each other. The positive in­ternal space charge (Fig. 5b) approaches the negatively charged extended SCR near the AEM; the negative internal space charge ap­proaches the CEM, where there is a positively charged extended SCR. Space-charge breakdown occurs when the distance between two oppo­sitely charged regions is sufficiently small and these space charges are short-circuited. The difference in comparison with the space-charge breakdown phenomenon described recently in our paper [15] is in the fact that in the previous work, the presence of H+ and OH-ions was not taken into account. The space charges of opposite sign were formed only by the salt ions as the extensions of the electric double layers adjacent to the membranes that form the desalination compartment [15]. For a short circuit to occur, the oppositely charged regions had to cover the entire distance between the membranes, whereas when water dissocia­tion takes place, the oppositely charged regions pass only half the intermembrane distance. Summarizing this section, it can be said that there is an interplay between chemical reactions and space charge in electrochemical sys­tems. On the one hand, space charge can affect chemical reactions.

citation.title: Mathematical modeling of electrodialysis of a dilute solution with accounting for water dissociation-recombination reactions

content: is an interplay between chemical reactions and space charge in electrochemical sys­tems. On the one hand, space charge can affect chemical reactions. Several mechanisms are possible. It is well known that dissociation of water molecules is accelerated by a strong electric field (the second Wien effect theoretically described by Onsager [74]). The intensity of the electric field is very high in the electric double layer at an ionexchange membrane, and especially at the junction of a bipolar mem­brane where it can reach 109 V/m [75–77]. Another mechanism is described by Rubinstein and Zaltzman [72]. They have shown that the space charge at an electrode can affect essentially the kinetics of a chemical (electrochemical) reaction at the electrode surface through electroconvection. Electroconvection increases the mass transfer rate of ion transport towards the electrode surface, which leads to an increase in the distance between the growth points of crystalline deposits on the electrode surface. The latter have a dendritic (like a tree) shape with several branches. Electroosmosis effectively stirs the solution between two dendritic branches, slowing down or eliminating their growth. On the other hand, an opposite effect described in this paper is possible: an intensive chemical reaction (recombination of H+ and OH-ions) can lead to the appearance of a space charge in the bulk solution in an ED desalination compartment. 2.3. 2D model 2.3.1.

Model formulation As in Section 2.1, a desalination chamber formed by an AEM and a CEM is considered. However, in this section, this chamber is a channel through which a binary salt (KCl) solution is flowing with an average velocity V (Fig. 6). The inlet solution concentration is C0, the pH is 7. The potential difference between x = 0 and x = H increases linearly with time: Δφ(t) = -d ⋅ t, where d is the sweep rate of potential difference growth; d = 5 mV/s in the cases considered below. The 2D model applied in this study is a generalization of the “basic” model developed earlier [15,17]. The model is based on the NernstPlanck-Poisson (NPP) equations with convective term together with the Navier-Stokes equation. The transport of four kinds of ions is described: K+, Cl-, H+ and OH-. These two blocks of equations are coupled through the electric volume force, f→ e, and fluid velocity, V→. f→ e is proportional to the space charge density and the electric field strength ( f→ e = ρ⋅E→= -ρ⋅∇φ), which are included in the NPP equations. When a SCR moves under the action of the electric force, this causes a convective transport of the solution (electroconvection) and affects the solution velocity, which is described by the Navier-Stokes equation. The change in V→influences ionic fluxes, which is taken into account by the NPP equations.

Such a curvature generates a normal component of ion fluxes directed towards and away from the membrane surface. Effect of the water dissociation/recombination reactions on the rate of desalination As in the case of the 1D model (Fig. 5a), the phenomenon of the “inversion” of the concentration profiles in the desalination compart­ment occurs. It takes place when the degree of desalination is very high and the concentrations of H+ and OH-ions predominate over the salt ion concentrations. Instead of the maximum salt concentration in the center of the desalination compartment, which occurs in conventional ED, there is a depletion region in the center. content: Desalination 550 (2023) 116398 reactions are taken into account, the concentration of K+ ions equal to 3 * 10-3 mol m-3 is already reached at a length of 400 μm, whereas in the case when these reactions are not taken into account, a channel with a length of 1000 μm is required to achieve the same concentration. This result is quite unexpected. The generation of H+ and OH-ions is an undesirable effect in the process of ED desalination. Water dissociation products compete with salt ions in charge transfer across the mem­branes, which reduces current efficiency [3,4,12,16,78–80]; pH shift provokes precipitation of sparingly soluble salts (scaling) [81,82] as well as the formation of organic deposit (fouling) [83,84]. In addition, H+ and OH-ions, which appear as a result of water splitting, reduce the space charge in the near-membrane solution, which suppresses elec­troconvection [58,85]. It is established experimentally that in the con­centration range close to 0.02 mol L-1 (20 mol m-3), the limiting and overlimiting salt transfer rate in electrodialysis decreases with increasing water splitting [86,87]. Water splitting and electroconvection are antagonists: the greater the rate of water splitting, the lower the electroconvection; and, conversely, intensive electroconvection reduces water splitting (due to delivery of the fresh solution from the bulk to the depleted near-membrane region of the solution).

content: electroconvection reduces water splitting (due to delivery of the fresh solution from the bulk to the depleted near-membrane region of the solution). What are the reasons why the above relationships between water splitting and mass transfer rate are not hold in the range of very dilute solutions? An analysis of the results of mathematical modeling allows us to identify two reasons: (1) Kharkats' exaltation effect [88,89] occurring due to water splitting, and (2) enhanced electroconvection due to space charge formed in the bulk solution.

The water ions generated in water dissociation at a membrane sur­face affect the electric field in the depleted solution and, consequently, the salt ion fluxes. Thus, H+ ions, which are formed at the AEM and released into the depleted solution, attract the salt anions from the bulk towards the membrane surface. As a result, the migration flux of salt anion (Cl-) towards the AEM increases, or becomes “exalted” in the words in the papers on membrane electrochemistry of Kharkats [88,89] and earlier studies on the electrode kinetics of Kemula and Michalski [90]. Similarly, OH-ions generated at the CEM into the depleted solu­tion attract the salt cations towards this membrane. The enhancement of overlimiting salt counterion flux through an IEM by the exaltation effect is described in the case of a 1:1 electrolyte and neutral bulk solution (pH = 7) by a simple relation [91,92]: j1 = 2D1Cb1δ+ D1 D4 jC4s,j2 = 2D2Cb2δ+ D2

Apparently, electroconvection is not efficient in terms of increasing mass transfer, if the H+ and OH-ions, formed during water splitting, enter the nearmembrane space regions and reduce the space charge density there. 3. Conclusion In the paper, for the first time, the effect of the dissociation/recom­bination reactions of water molecules on the transfer of salt ions during ED of highly dilute solutions is theoretically analyzed. It is shown that under such conditions, the products of water dissociation can dominate over salt ions and can be potential-determining. In this case, in the central part of the desalination channel, an electrical double layer (EDL) arises due to the recombination reaction of H+ and OH-ions. Between this internal EDL and the near-membrane space charge region, an electrically neutral region can be located. At a high degree of desali­nation, when the H+ and OH-ions predominate over the salt ions, the phenomenon of “inversion” of the shape of concentration profiles in the desalination compartment is observed. Instead of the maximum salt concentration in the center of the desalination compartment, which occurs in conventional ED, there is a depletion region. The appearance of this region is due to water dissociation/recombination reactions and the tendency of the system to electroneutrality.

citation.title: Mathematical modeling of electrodialysis of a dilute solution with accounting for water dissociation-recombination reactions

content: letion region. The appearance of this region is due to water dissociation/recombination reactions and the tendency of the system to electroneutrality. The H+ and OH-ions generated at the surface of CEM and AEM, retain salt ions in the nearmembrane regions of the solution. With that, the recombination of H+ and OH-ions occurs in the center of the compartment. The very low concentration of these ions (close to 10-7 mol L-1) does not allow them to retain salt ions. In the near-membrane solution regions, the dissociation reaction prevails over the recombination reaction, due to the fact that in these areas the electric field strength takes on such large values that the electric field removes H+ and OH-ions from the reaction zone, pre­venting them from recombine. The recombination of H+ and OH-ions occurs at the center of the desalination channel in the vicinity of a point (denoted as point xr), where the product of H+ and OH-concentrations attains a maximum, which exceeds the water dissociation constant Kw. Although this reaction is local, all H+ and OH-ions cannot simulta­neously recombine at one point. There is an excess of H+ ions in the left semi-neighborhood of the xr point, and an excess of OH-ions in its right semi-neighborhood; thus, an EDL is formed in the bulk solution. The use of 2D modeling showed that the appearance of an internal EDL signifi­cantly affects the spatio-temporal ion concentration and fluid flow patterns.

content: When water splitting occurs, the H+/OH-ions affects the electric field in diffusion layer, which results in an increase in salt counterion fluxes towards the membrane (the exaltation effect). In conventional ED desalination of moderately dilute solutions, the contribution of the exaltation effect is insignificant. However, when the feed solution is very dilute, this effect becomes important. It is found that when the con­centrations of the salt and water ions are comparable in the desalination channel, the exaltation effect can provide a 50 % contribution to the total salt ion transfer through the membrane. As a result, the partial current of salt ions through the membranes at relatively low voltages is higher in the channel where water splitting occurs compared to the channel, where this reaction is not taken into account. However, un­stable electroconvection, which develops at large voltages, is more efficient (in terms of mass transfer enhancement) in a channel without water splitting, since H+/OH-ions reduce the near-membrane space charge. In addition, H+ and OH-ions generated during water splitting are located near ion-exchange membranes, where they retain salt ions. The situation is such that in the middle of the desalination channel, the concentration of H+ and OH-ions is low, since they recombine there. The concentration of salt ions there is also very low, whereas the ions are accumulated near the membranes.

citation.title: Mathematical modeling of electrodialysis of a dilute solution with accounting for water dissociation-recombination reactions

content: H-ions is low, since they recombine there. The concentration of salt ions there is also very low, whereas the ions are accumulated near the membranes. This ion distribution is inverse compared to conventional ED, where the ion concentration is relatively high in the center of the channel, and near the membranes, the ion concentration is very low (concentration polarization). Moreover, the fact that H+ and OH-ions are continuously generated and they retain salt ions in the channel means that it is impossible to remove all salt ions from feed water during its electrodialysis treatment. It should be noted that the models presented in this paper describe water splitting under the assumption of the lowest possible rate of water dissociation (the influence of the electric field and the catalytic partic­ipation of fixed functional groups are not taken into account). In real conditions, the rate of water splitting would be significantly greater, as well as its effect on the ED desalination process.
